Go-to-Market
Snapshot:
The CMO Game

I developed this GTM snapshot after playing The CMO Game in Prof. Marr's Advanced Marketing class, where Andrew Mitrak came to the class in person. I used Claude to help build and stress-test the strategy, then revised where AI got it wrong.

Product
cmogame.com
Creator
Andrew Mitrak
Launched
Feb 2026
Price
Free, No Login
01Product Overview

The CMO Game is a free browser-based marketing strategy simulator with a retro pixel-art aesthetic that immediately signals: this is a game, not a training module. You play as Director of Marketing with a $5M annual budget and 12 months to reach the C-suite. You pick a product (soda, shoes, skincare, or software), lock in a positioning strategy, build your team, and navigate monthly budget allocations while managing CEO pressure, brand equity, and audience reactions. Unexpected events hit mid-game, from industry conferences to PR crises, and your prior spending decisions affect the outcome. If you overindex on performance and ignore brand, you feel it by Month 8. A full playthrough takes about 15 minutes. A Hall of Fame leaderboard ranks the top 100 players globally, driving replay through competition rather than completion.

Customer Problem
Marketing is widely taught through lectures, frameworks, and case studies. None of these simulate the organizational pressure of managing budgets under CEO scrutiny or defending brand equity decisions. The CMO Game fills this gap by putting learners inside those decisions without real career consequences, at no cost.
Category and Competition
Marketing strategy simulator. Complements Harvard Business Publishing simulations (expensive and institutional) and passive formats like podcasts and books. The CMO Game is one of the few free, frictionless, decision-under-pressure options in this category.
Monthly budget allocation with sliders across Brand Building, Performance Marketing, PR and Influencers, and Events. Prior decisions compound: underinvest in brand early and you feel it by Month 8.
Mid-game unexpected event: the Skincare Symposium expo. Prior spending on Events unlocks bonus samples. This is where the game stops feeling like a simulator and starts feeling like a real judgment call under pressure.
The Hall of Fame: top 100 marketing legends worldwide. The same player appears multiple times with different products and strategies. The leaderboard drives replay: each run is a new strategic bet, not a repeated tutorial.
02Target Audience and Customer Insight

When I finished playing, my instinct was to share it with my MBA classmates entering marketing roles. That reaction is itself a data point about who this product is for.

Audience Profile
Age 24 to 32. In or recently graduated from an MBA or graduate marketing program, or in their first marketing role. Understands marketing vocabulary (brand equity, performance marketing, in-house vs. agency) but has never managed a real budget under organizational pressure.
Core Pain Point
They can define marketing frameworks fluently. They have never had a CEO ask them why revenue is down. The gap between knowing marketing and leading it is not closed by more theory. It requires simulated experience under pressure.
Why Smart Starting Point
Concentrated in MBA programs where professors are the natural distribution channel. Prof. Marr's course use validated this model. Active LinkedIn users who share career content organically. Enough marketing literacy to appreciate the game's depth without being confused by it.
Key Assumption
This audience is primarily US-based and English-speaking. A second wave targeting international markets, particularly India and Southeast Asia where MBA marketing programs are growing rapidly, would require a different channel strategy. This GTM focuses on the US launch window.
Market Sizing
Total Addressable Market
9.4M
All marketing professionals on LinkedIn globally who could benefit from strategy simulation practice. Includes practitioners, academics, and career changers entering marketing.
Serviceable Addressable Market
450K
US-based marketing MBA students and early-career marketers (1 to 3 years experience) actively seeking career development tools. English-speaking, digitally active, and concentrated in reachable academic and professional communities.
Serviceable Obtainable Market
15K
Reachable in the first 90 days through targeted outreach to professors at 50 MBA programs combined with Andrew Mitrak's existing LinkedIn following and podcast audience. At an average of 50 students per course adoption, 15 adoptions delivers approximately 750 engaged players with high sharing intent.
03Positioning and Messaging
Positioning Statement
For marketing MBA students and early-career marketers preparing for their first leadership role, The CMO Game is a free marketing strategy simulator that puts you inside the organizational pressure of real marketing decisions (budget trade-offs, CEO expectations, and audience dynamics) because the gap between knowing marketing and leading it is only closed by doing.
Headlines and Message Directions
01You've studied marketing. Now survive it.Selected
Getting fired is a genuine outcome. The pixel-art gravestone lands with enough dark humor that players immediately want to retry. It makes the "survive it" headline literal: this is not a safe space for bad strategy. That consequence is the product's core value.
0212 months. $5M. One angry CEO. Can you make CMO?
03The gap between knowing marketing and doing marketing, closed in 20 minutes.
04Not a certificate. A global rank. The CMO Game puts your marketing strategy on a leaderboard against players worldwide.
"Mitrak's game sparks the right conversations by not giving you marketing answers, but better questions to ask, which is where real learning starts."
Elton X. Graham, CMO, Sur La Table
Why the Leaderboard Changes the Value Proposition
Most marketing simulations end with a certificate. The CMO Game ends with a global rank. A leaderboard entry is more useful in a job interview than a completion badge: it shows the specific strategy you played, the revenue you generated, and your brand equity score, all benchmarked against real players worldwide. It is also competitive in a way certificates are not, which is why the same players appear multiple times in the top 100, each entry representing a different strategic bet. The learning is not in finishing. It is in coming back and doing it differently.
Prof. Marr uses The CMO Game to open his Advanced Marketing course, describing it as "a great way to break the ice in the first class." I was in that class session, and Andrew Mitrak came to the class in person.
Source: Andrew Mitrak, A History of Marketing ↗
Source: Andrew Mitrak's launch post for The CMO Game, marketinghistory.org
What I Accepted, Edited, and Rejected from AI
AI Suggested (Rejected)
Positioned the CMO Game as "edtech" alongside LinkedIn Learning and Coursera. Messaging emphasized skill certifications and course completion. Recommended headline: "Master Marketing Strategy with The CMO Game." Treated the absence of a certificate as a product weakness.
Human Revision (Kept)
Repositioned as a strategy simulator. "Master" implies a credential. "Survive" implies pressure. Pressure is the product. The leaderboard rank is a stronger credential than any certificate: it is competitive, global, and conversation-starting. AI missed this entirely. Accepted: the positioning template structure and the speed of headline iteration (eight variations tested in minutes).
04Lightweight GTM Plan
Marketing MBA Programs
Professors are the distribution layer. One professor adoption delivers the game to 30 to 60 students in a single class session at zero acquisition cost. Prof. Marr's use case is a validated, replicable template. Target: marketing department chairs and professors at the top 50 US MBA programs.
Andrew Mitrak's Owned Platforms
Andrew is Senior Manager at Google Cloud AI Demand Labs with 5,960 LinkedIn followers and an active posting cadence. His podcast "A History of Marketing" features guests including Philip Kotler, April Dunford, and Guy Kawasaki, who each bring large senior marketing audiences. A creator who is employed at Google, runs a respected marketing history podcast, and built the game in his own time carries credibility that no paid placement can replicate. When guests share the game with their own networks, it reaches CMOs and marketing leaders who are the exact people the target audience wants to impress on their leaderboard.
LinkedIn Player Sharing
The target audience already shares career-relevant content on LinkedIn. Players naturally want to share scores and strategic decisions. A structured end-of-game sharing prompt converts every player into a distribution node, creating a growing organic signal that reaches the exact audience most likely to play and share again.
Launch Tactics
Tactic 1: Class Competition Format
Pitch professors not on a game, but on a structured class competition. Students play individually, then the class compares leaderboard rankings and debates their strategic decisions. "Which team hit the highest revenue, and why?" generates post-game analysis that makes the learning permanent. Prof. Marr's session is the validated template. The leaderboard turns a solo 15-minute game into a 45-minute class discussion with real strategic stakes. Estimated conversion: 1 in 4 professors pitched runs a class competition within 30 days.
Tactic 2: Score-Sharing Prompt
At game completion, prompt players with a rank-anchored LinkedIn post: "I just hit Rank [X] in the CMO Game Hall of Fame. I chose [product] with a [positioning] strategy and generated [revenue] in 12 months. My biggest mistake: [X]. Think you can beat it? cmogame.com" Rank is more provocative than revenue alone. It implies an open challenge. A specific rank in a global leaderboard is a stronger conversation starter than a revenue number because it invites comparison and debate, which is exactly the kind of engagement that reaches new players organically.
MK
Maitri K.
Marketing MBA Candidate | UI/UX and Advanced Marketing

Mockup: what a rank-based share looks like. The specific rank creates an open challenge that drives comments, debate, and new players.
90-Day Launch Sequence
Weeks 1 to 2Outreach to 20 marketing professors at target MBA programs via LinkedIn, citing Prof. Marr's use case and offering Andrew a virtual class session.
Weeks 3 to 6Andrew attends or connects with 3 to 5 class sessions. Each is run as a competition: students play individually, then compare leaderboard ranks and debate their decisions as a class. The discussion is where the learning embeds. Each session produces players who post their rank on LinkedIn and challenge classmates to beat it.
Weeks 4 to 8Andrew features the game on his podcast, asks guests to share it with their networks. High-profile guests (CMOs, professors) reaching senior marketing audiences create earned amplification.
Weeks 5 to 8Track LinkedIn organic posts. Optimize the score-sharing prompt based on which result types drive the most engagement and reshares.
Weeks 9 to 12Second wave of 20 additional professor outreaches using social proof from wave one adoptions. Each new adopting professor becomes a reference for the next.
KPI 1: 90-Day Target
15 courses
MBA or graduate marketing programs formally incorporating the game. Measures institutional reach and validates the professor distribution channel. Each adoption is a multiplier: one professor equals up to 60 new players.
KPI 2: 90-Day Target
35% replay rate
Percentage of first-time players who return for a second run within 14 days. This is a deeper signal than post counts or completions. A 35% replay rate means players are returning to test a different strategy, which is the core learning behavior the product is designed to drive. It also validates the leaderboard as a retention mechanic and predicts sustained word-of-mouth as players share improving scores over time.
05AI Process and Reflection
Primary Prompt Used
"I'm developing a GTM snapshot for The CMO Game, a free browser-based marketing strategy simulator by Andrew Mitrak. Target audience: marketing students and early-career marketers. The creator has no paid acquisition budget but has an active LinkedIn presence and a podcast with senior marketing guests. Generate: (1) positioning statement, (2) three headline options, (3) two to three launch channels and why they fit, (4) two 90-day KPIs. Do not recommend paid acquisition."
See Full AI Response ▾

AI Positioning (generated): "For marketing professionals and aspiring CMOs who want to sharpen their strategic thinking, The CMO Game is the only free marketing strategy game that lets you practice CMO-level decisions, because the best way to learn is to live through the consequences of your choices."

AI Headlines: 1. "Master Marketing Strategy with The CMO Game" / 2. "Think Like a CMO: Free Marketing Simulation" / 3. "Level Up Your Marketing Career in 20 Minutes"

AI Channels: Content marketing and SEO targeting "marketing career" keywords. LinkedIn Learning-style newsletter partnerships with Marketing Brew. Podcast episode promotion through Andrew's existing show.

AI KPIs: Monthly active users. Course completion rate among players who start the game.

What AI Helped With
The positioning statement template structure and rapid headline iteration. Generating eight headline variations in minutes surfaced the rank-based angle ("Not a certificate. A global rank.") faster than manual drafting would have. AI also correctly flagged Andrew's podcast as a distribution channel and identified named executive endorsements as primary credibility signals. Both were sound and kept.
What I Did Not Trust AI to Do
Define the audience. AI suggested "marketing professionals and aspiring CMOs," which is accurate but too broad to drive real channel decisions. The insight that the right launch audience is people between learning marketing and having to lead it came from playing the game in Prof. Marr's class and noticing which moments landed hardest with my classmates: specifically the budget pressure and CEO reactions, not the tactics.
Ethical and Accuracy Risks
AI generated an unverified market size statistic for "marketing simulation software." Removed entirely: unverified claims create credibility risk in a strategy document. AI also suggested framing the game as a direct competitor to Harvard Business Publishing simulations. Changed to "complements" since Andrew Mitrak's goal is to supplement, not replace, existing marketing education. Critically, AI treated the absence of a completion certificate as a product weakness and recommended adding one. This is the wrong instinct entirely. The Hall of Fame leaderboard is a stronger credential: it is competitive, specific, global, and conversation-starting. A player who holds Rank 12 worldwide has something more compelling to say in a job interview than someone who completed a course module. Recognizing the leaderboard as the product's actual retention and credibility mechanism changed both KPI 2 and the sharing prompt from generic awareness metrics to genuinely differentiated strategic choices.
